Hey everyone. I've been quiet for a while, but I finally have something to show for my time working on my language Mech the last couple of months. Last I showed it off here I had a video demo of a bouncing balls program. Shortly after that video I tore everything out and made a more realistic runtime. After about 4 months of work I'm back to where I was then, feature wise, but now the foundation is more stable and you can actually play with it in the browser:

<http://mech-lang.gitlab.io/try/>

Click compile and the bouncing ball program compiles. Then click start timer to set the balls in motion. You can click in the canvas area to add more balls. Click pause to stop them. Step forward and back moves through the transaction history.

I don't really have a user guide done yet, but I have a list of expression that I know work, so you can get an idea of what's possible from this: <https://gitlab.com/mech-lang/syntax/blob/master/tests/compiler.rs>

The main idea is that each block of code reads from or writes to a set of global tables, denoted by hashtags. When all the input to a block is available, that block will execute the operations defined therein, and then writes data out to the global tables with a set operator `:=`. Blocks re-execute every time its inputs change, providing a reactive, live feel. You can also gate a block's execution on a specific column with the `~` operator, which will cause a block to run _only_ if the associated column changes.

Right now, there are a ton of language features I haven't implemented yet. What I have implemented are basic math, operators, some inequality operators, logic operators, table construction syntax, and indexing operators. I'd like to make sure these operators are rock solid before I move on to anything else. I'm hoping some intrepid people here can test drive what I have now to find cases that don't work. For now, you can see program output in the Chrome console by clicking on "View Core". If you find any code that explodes or produces unexpected output, please let me know by filing an issue here: <https://github.com/mech-lang/mech/issues>

text: Dear Ms. Solstad,  if your abstract algebra is good, programming will be easy. Frankly programming uses very little math; the necessary pre-requisites are not taught well in school however; you can go through calculus and not be exposed to what they call "discrete math", which includes set theory, boolean algebra, combinatorics, etc.  You are entering the field at an odd time in history, because programming was once easier, but more limited. Now, with billions of computers around in all sizes, programming has become very challenging, and the haphazard way the dominant programming stacks were created has resulted in a huge mess. Javascript, which by many measures is the #1 language is a "hot mess" as they call it. We are at what i perceive was the tail end of OOP era, and are at the boundary of a whole new, much easier era, So it is a great time to jump in if you look forward a bit. One of the best ways to learn is to build something very simple. Find the absolutely simplest thing you can imagine, and do that. My first program was finding the area of a triangle. Motivation precedes achievement, and since you want to make more human things, perhaps you can apply your knowledge of stage directing and acting to think of some tool that would benefit actors to memorize lines, or tools synchronize lighting cues, or something that combines your real world knowledge, and programming. It has been somewhat tragic that computer programmers are often in charge of figuring out what to build, when they don't have knowledge of the outside world. You can be a very successful programmer, and never leave your bathrobe and slippers.
This is very interesting, thank you for sharing!

I really like the sidebar that shows possible actions depending on the current context. This makes exploring the tool much easier. But I also agree with Steve that a video / gif showing the basic functionality would be helpful.
I did some experiments with structured editing myself last year (<https://github.com/fkohlgrueber/vrite>), but didn't get that far...
I think that one reason structured editing hasn't become popular might be that most structured editors are tightly coupled to a specific language. People use different languages all the time and learning new interactions for each language would be too much effort. It would probably be interesting to try to develop interactions that work well with all kinds of languages.

Thanks, I hope I find some time to make a video next week (dayjob keeping me busy :see_no_evil:). And great to see that your exploring that space in a similar way!
RE supporting different languages: I agree that ultimately such an editor should support multiple languages, but I don't think that's the reason why it hasn't gotten popular. My theory for that is that few structural editors focus enough on the editing experience. Imo the only one that got close is Paredit (unfortunately they solved it for a niche-y language). My masterplan (as Steve called it in the podcast I just listened to) would be to prove it first for a popular lang, like JS, and use the learnings from that to build something more general purpose

My masterplan (as Steve called it in the podcast I just listened to) would be to prove it first for a popular lang, like JS, and use the learnings from that to build something more general purpose
Sounds like a good plan! I agree that focusing on a simple but popular language first and adding additional languages later is probably the best way to tackle this problem.

I agree in the sense I feel 'having or not having' is a very different thing than 'int or string' and fusing them into the same type system seems off. I'm also more comfortable with the set oriented thinking that Hickey discusses.

Relatedly, I'm also not a fan of the usual ADT types anymore. I think they impose too much of an implementation detail onto what should be a conceptually pure relationship model. They define not just the relationship but also the bit representation and normalization/denormalization strategy. I feel the entities and relationships should be defined separately from how they materialize - what parts are in contiguous bits of memory, whether they are row oriented or column oriented, etc.

> I feel the entities and relationships should be defined separately from how they materialize

Clojure is actually pretty good for this. The abundant data manipulation functions are all coded to interfaces that just care about things like "Is this sorted? Is this a seq? It it associative?". You can add your own concrete datatype with, say, a different storage strategy, and conform to whichever interfaces make sense. Very a la carte.

The demo works beautifully and absolutely nails that "exploratory feel". I'm not exactly sure what causes it, but it has that same feeling as Excel that playing around is safe, which is why it is popular with non-programmers (in fact, it's actually possible to get yourself completely muddled in Excel and lose data, but it doesn't *feel* like it).

The demo works beautifully and absolutely nails that "exploratory feel". I'm not exactly sure what causes it, but it has that same feeling as Excel that playing around is safe, which is why it is popular with non-programmers (in fact, it's actually possible to get yourself completely muddled in Excel and lose data, but it doesn't *feel* like it).

The only thing I would say is what I always feel about visual tools - that I wish it was also writing code underneath, that I could then go and edit. The search works great, but how do I search for long names, names with no 'e's etc.

I think there should always be the option to switch to "real" programming, it should just never be at the expense of the natural visual interface

Some of the strengths and (common) weaknesses of declarative programming: <https://www.toptal.com/software/declarative-programming>

attachments.title: Declarative Programming: Is It A Real Thing?
attachments.text: In a nutshell, declarative programming consists of instructing a program on what needs to be done, instead of telling it how to do it. This approach involves providing a domain-specific language (DSL) for expressing what the user wants. This DSL shields users from messy low-level constructs while still achieving the desired end-state. While declarative programming offers advantages over the imperative approach it replaces, it’s not as straightforward as it may seem. In this comprehensive article, Toptal Freelance Software Engineer Federico Pereiro outlines his experience with declarative tools and explains how you can make declarative programming work for you.
attachments.fallback: Toptal Engineering Blog: Declarative Programming: Is It A Real Thing?
